text: We present a case of a fifty-five-year-old female who had a posterior repair in the past and presented with a recurrent rectocele. She also had symptoms of obstructive defecation with evidence of recto-rectal interception and internal prolapse. The patient is placed in modified Lloyd-Davis position on an anti-slip mattress. The ports are shown as below. The optical ten millimeter port at the umbilicus and two pores on the right side and one in the left eyelid.

The surgeon begins the procedure with a trans-abdominal stitch using a straight proline to elevate the uterus and open the Douglas pouch. The sacral promontory is clearly visible, and the right ureter is visualized. Dissection is performed using monopolar diathermy on scissors, creating a peritoneal incision that allows CO₂ to dissect the tissue planes. A clearly defined plane is developed behind the mesorectal package, with careful identification and preservation of the hypogastric nerves.

The surgeon performs dissection in a craniocaudal direction along the right border of the rectum. The incision is extended anteriorly to open the rectal vaginal septum, revealing the back wall of the vagina. Proper traction is applied to the rectum to open the peritoneal fold, ensuring the avascular plane is entered without damaging the rectum or vagina.

The surgeon uses their left hand to control and manipulate the rectum from right to left, cutting through scar tissue in the retropaginal septum without placing retractors in the vagina. Dissection continues down to the level of the perineal body, where the extent of dissection is assessed using a year-end's crasper on the rectum during a rectal examination.

The surgeon performs precise dissection using monopolar diathermy, carefully avoiding thermal damage to the rectal and vaginal walls. The extent of dissection is evaluated, reaching approximately three to four centimeters from the anal verge, at the top of the anal canal. An IPOM polypropylene mesh, measuring fifteen by five centimeters, is secured to the anterior rectal wall with four Ethicon IIO sutures. The sutures are placed to capture sufficient muscular area without full thickness penetration of the rectal wall, minimizing infection risk. The intracorporeal stitching is performed in a narrow space.

The surgeon places four sutures approximately one centimeter apart to secure the mesh to the anterior rectal wall. A 30-degree camera is utilized to aid in suturing deep within the pelvis, with two needle holders used to ensure precision and prevent loose suture ends.

The surgeon secures the proximal end of the mesh to the sacral promontory using tackers, ensuring careful placement to avoid the inferior mesenteric artery. The tension on the mesh is adjusted to prevent prolapse recurrence while maintaining proper anorectal angle for continence.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal fold over the mesh using a running suture of 2-0 PDS, ensuring the mesh is completely covered and positioned extraperitoneally.
